speaker: interviewer
text: --with Dr. Ruth Steckl. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How was your departure from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was pretty bad. We left, as I said originally, by train from Prague. And we went to the station at 8 o'clock and stood at the station between 8:00 and 12:00. We left at midnight. And all the families were standing on the station. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] It's so many years, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] but I still get sick when I think of-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I'm sorry. Well, I never forget the faces of my parents standing there, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not knowing whether we'll meet again, [CRYING] which obviously we didn't. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, finally we left. And it took about eight hours to get to Bratislava. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was really unfortunate that we were so close. From my hometown, it was probably not more than six hours, but we never could get together anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only way we kept in contact was by letters at that time. One didn't phone yet. So we-- letters were censored, but they got there. And so we had-- they couldn't send any money, like I said before. But somehow they managed by finding somebody who went there and who had somebody who could get the money and then give it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think we still had the same-- I don't remember what currency we had. But I think we had the same currency, even through the war. I-- honest to God, I don't know. But I believe it was the same currency. Probably not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What took place after the Bratislava internment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's actually a funny story. In August of '39-- in August of 1940, all our money apparently had run out. And we were all cold. And we were all told that we have to pack our things and we have to go back. And so there was tremendous excitement, obviously. And somehow, the last minute, we were saved, because there was a transport planned from Vienna with a Mr. [? Stouffer ?], who somehow had agreed to add our 320 people to his transport. I-- really, I mean, I was too young at that time to understand how the whole thing worked. But we were told the next day or maybe even the same day that everything is OK, don't worry, because there was tremendous excitement when they told us that we had to pack.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were waiting for that boat to arrive. And at the same time, there was another coincident-- coincidence. There were Germans-- ethnic Germans who lived in Bessarabia were slated to come back to the Reich. And they needed transportation for them. And since the steamers who would take the people from Bessarabia to Germany went, you know, on the Danube and they would have had to go back empty, they decided that it was a good idea to take the Jews and let them pay for that trip, which normally would have cost them and now they could pay for the whole trip themselves, I mean with our money. So that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on September 3rd, I believe, 3rd or 6th, two steamers arrived in Bratislava, the and the The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was carrying people from Vienna. And the was carrying 500 people from Gdansk, Danzig. And we were assigned to the And on September 3rd, we finally left Bratislava and got on the The Danzig people there were not very happy to see us, because the ship was terribly crowded. It was unbelievable. It was actually a very nice boat. It was, I think, relatively new. But there were the 500 people from Danzig plus the 300-odd people from the Czech transport. And there was no place to be found. It was awful. I still remember we were running up and down the ship trying to find a sleeping place. Nothing. People were so crowded they were standing on top of each other. And I remember finally I think at 3 o'clock at night I found a place under a table and that's where I slept. And I was very happy that nobody stepped on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the conditions on the ship were not really very good. Food was poor and really insufficient. Washing facilities. You can imagine for 800 people on a small boat. It-- it was-- it was not very pleasant.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, it was-- we had beautiful weather. I still remember that. And the sights were lovely. I mean, we passed Budapest and Belgrade and the Iron Gate. And that was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took about a week to come down to Tulcea, where our sea-- sea-- what do you say?-- seafaring ship waited for us, the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's another story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My god, when I remember when we first saw the it was an old, decrepit, Greek freighter. It looked awful. It was flying the Panama flag.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after-- about three days after we arrived in Tulcea, we went aboard the And I'll never forget [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we first got on there what it looked like. First of all, it smelled terrible. Second, we were 1,800 people on that boat, which, you know, consisted of the transport from the and the transport from the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I think that's All.  But I know there were 1,800 people. And the ship probably had place maybe for 200 or 300. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were very few cabins. And the cabins were usually occupied. There were a few older people. Actually, the Czech transport had no-- no old people. We were all young. There were a couple of maybe middle-aged couples, but that was all. Most of the people were in their either teens or 20s or maybe early 30s. But the people from-- from Danzig, they had quite a few old people. And also the people from the which-- who were Viennese had quite a few old people. So some of the cabins were assigned to the older people. And we just-- I remember I-- I don't think I had a sleeping place for probably four days. We were just, you know, running around and-- and bumping into people, shoving and pushing, and just lay down somewhere. Only after several days we found-- I found a place with a friend of mine. There were some bunkers and some planks down there. It was-- we called it the catacombs. And there we-- we finally could put our luggage down. We had a rucksack and a little small suitcase. And sort of-- I-- I think we had-- we had a blanket and there was some straw on-- on those planks. But it was terrible. There were I don't know how many people in that bunker. And you could hear everybody. You could hear everything that was going on, you could-- the first time I saw sex or heard sex, which was not very pleasant, I can tell you that. And there we stayed for quite a while. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- the sanitary conditions were terrible. The food was lousy. Water was insufficient. And it was really bad. I remember we had some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some provisions which we brought from Bratislava, which we were keeping obviously for a time that it was absolutely necessary to add something to what we were getting. But I remember one day we were so hungry, so that I decided-- I had a box of cookies which my parents had sent me to Bratislava, which was probably three-- three months or four months old. But I decided with a friend of mine, well, to hell with it, let's eat those cookies.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I opened the box. And I looked-- when we looked at the box, it was full with worms. So what we did-- we were so hungry. We went to a corner which was very dark, and it was very easy to find on that boat, and we ate those cookies.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget that. They were very good, even though they were full with worms. And nothing really detrimental happened to us from the cookies. But otherwise, the sanitary conditions on the ship were really very bad. And quite a few people started getting sick and started with diarrhea and high fever. And we were already starting to murmur about, you know, typhoid or dysentery. And I myself got sick once. I was-- I got a high fever and diarrhea. And it happened after I had helped a friend of mine, or a lady actually who was very nice to me usually. I had helped her wash the underwear from her husband who was sick. And next day, I got sick. And so they said, oh my god, you know. Better go to the hospital. So they had a little hospital on the boat, so they shipped me to the hospital. And that hospital was about the worst thing I have ever seen in my life. There were bunkbeds, three. And I-- they put me on the middle bunkbed, and you couldn't sit up. You could barely turn over. And there was some nursing care, but the nurses were busy. There were quite a few really sick people there, so I felt I wasn't really sick enough to stay there, so I ran away. That was-- and fortunately I-- I was OK the next day or a few days later. Yeah. The-- the condition on the ship were terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The washing facilities were impossible. You had to-- what you did all day, you were standing in line for something. You were standing in line for food. You were standing in line for water. You were standing in line to wash up. You were standing in line for the latrines. And when you were finished with one line, so you had to start another one. And sometimes you just gave up and you didn't eat or you didn't wash. And sometimes you went for days without washing. I remember I-- once I had a sweater, which had a white collar. And I was walking on the boat somewhere and somebody was walking behind and told me, hey, I think you better wash your neck. I-- that was very unpleasant. That had never happened to me before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- the boat, we were about three weeks I believe in Tulcea before we sailed. And I have no idea why we stood there so long. But then we sailed. And we got to Istanbul, where we anchored for a couple of days. And actually, the Jews in Istanbul were very nice and they sent us some food, some bread, and-- and I think some-- some other stuff. And I think we stood there for a day or two and then we continued. And then we got to Crete, eventually. And we stopped there in Heraklion. And unfortunately, it was on the eve of the breakout of the war between Italy and Greece. And so we were stuck again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stood on Crete for about three weeks. And during that time, the crew, who really consisted of not very honorable people, decided that it was not a good idea to sail during the time of war. So they burned part of our precious coal and threw the other part into the ocean and the captain refused to sail. So the-- our-- we had a group which was called the Haganah, which consisted of young men who tried to keep order on the boat. And so they took over and they manned the ship. And they put the cabin-- locked the captain into a cabin and they took over the ship. And they worked as engineers and stokers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was decided at that time that Palestine was too far, so they would go to Cyprus, because we didn't have enough coal. But even the shorter distance to Cyprus proved too long for the little coal that was there, so they started really stripping the ship. They took all the beds, all the mats, every piece of wood which was not absolutely necessary, and heated the ship with it. And the ship was floating on the water just like a metal skeleton. And that was terrible. That was really the only time we were frightened, because any time a storm could happen and we would have been done for. Incidentally, I remember one time prior to that that I was so desperate because of the way we-- we were sleeping and couldn't really breathe well. So I decided-- even though the weather was really bad, I decided to go on deck. And since the weather was very bad, there were very few people on deck, so I found a place to lay down, which was great. And I fell asleep, only to be awakened by the worst storm I have ever experienced. And I'll never forget that. It-- it was pouring rain. And I can only remember when I opened my eyes, the ship was going from side to side. The mast was going like this and like this. And I got desperately seasick, but really desperately. And I couldn't move and the rain was pouring. And I was lying there, just-- I thought, I'll-- I'll never survive this. And then I think after an hour or two somebody from the Haganah who had come up deck to look around-- because I think I was the only one there-- whether somebody was there found me and dragged me down. And I don't remember what happened after that, but that was one of the worst experiences of my life. Boy, I was so sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, we went-- so we decided to go to Cyprus. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we didn't really know-- I remember they didn't know where they were. It was all very confusing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the weather was also bad. And the ship was barely moving, because we didn't have enough coal. And so we never knew whether we were going to make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, apparently, if I remember, the coast of Cyprus appeared and everyone was rejoicing. And nobody really cared whether the British would see us or not, because we thought we would die. And British tug boats dragged us into-- into Cyprus, into Limassol harbor. And that's where we stood for a couple of days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remember there was a warship which accompanied-- accompanied us to Haifa. And so we arrived in Haifa sometime in the beginning of December or the end of November. I don't remember the date. And when we came to Haifa, there was a big ship, a really luxury liner standing in the harbor. It was under steam. And we were told that we were going to be put on that luxury liner in lieu of a quarantine, because Atlit, which usually served this quarantine, apparently was too busy or they couldn't take us or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't understand why the liner was under steam, but we were told it had to be under steam because of the food and of the water and of all the things that were needed. It was called the And that's another story. So we were supposed to be transported or transferred to the within the next couple of days. And they started with some of our people. And I don't remember exactly how many already got on But I and a friend of mine were standing on deck and we were the next one slated to go on one of the boats to be taken over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden there was a loud explosion and the went down. In five minutes, it was gone. And there was lots of wreckage and lots of people swimming and trying to get away from the waves. Yeah. That was terrible. My god. And a friend of mine who had a brother on the because the people from the other two boats, which I hadn't mentioned that there were other-- there were two other boats which came before us. They-- in Tulcea were three boats. One was the the other one was the and the third one was the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were three liners who waited for people like us. And there were two other transports who had come at the same time, one from Czechoslovakia on the and one from Vienna on the And so all together, the people who wanted to enter Palestine at that time numbered about 4,000. I forgot to mention that, which is very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those two boats, the the and the had arrived before us, because they didn't have any problems. And they already were on the Yeah. I'm sorry. That's very important. I forgot to mention that. So we were now supposed to be added to those people on the So the sank. And it-- we were told after that that it had been an act of sabotage, because they didn't want-- they had found out that they were going to be deported and they tried to avoid that by somehow wrecking the engines, because they knew that shipwrecked people usually are not being deported, or can't be deported, because there is such a law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the-- the Haganah in Israel had prepared for that sabotage. Unfortunately, they didn't do it very well. And instead of blowing up only the engines, they blew up part of the boat. They were supposed to do it when all the people are on deck so that nobody would get hurt and people could get into life boats or jump into the water and something, and only part of the ship would be wrecked. And I guess the bomb was too big or whatever they did just was too much. And so about 300-odd people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we clarify who the Haganah were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, the Haganah was the sort of pseudo-military organization of Zionists. And they were clandestine at that time, because of the British, obviously. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah. It was pretty bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, after a couple of days, we were all transported to Atlit, which was a camp in Israel which usually served as a quarantine for the immigrants. And we-- actually, we got to-- to Atlit and were put into huts. And actually, we were in a hut-- a friend of mine and I were in a hut together with the people from the rather than our people, just by pure chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was quite pleasant to be with other people, because we had been with the people from the Czech transport for so long. It was quite pleasant to be with somebody else. But-- and I had actually a couple of friends from my hometown who had come with one of the other boats, so I was in a hut with one of them, which was really very nice. However, after about four or five days, we were told that the people who had been on the and the people who had not been on the like us, had to be separated. And I still think that had we known what was going to happen, which we didn't, we could have stayed in that hut and nobody would have said anything and we could have stayed in Palestine, but we were stupid. I mean, we were young. They told us we had to go to a different hut, so we went. And soon after that, they started to erect barbed wire between those two camps, the ones on one side and the other one on the other side. On one side were the people who had been on the which were considered shipwrecked. And on the other side were people like me, who had not been on the yet and were not considered shipwrecked. And so it was decided to deport the people who had not been shipwrecked. And that was us. And so after two, three days on the other side of the camp, we were told that-- pack your things and tomorrow morning you're going to leave. You're going to be transported or deported to Mauritius.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop 'til the next--
